Longitudinal O*NET Archive Analysis

Source: vignettes/longitudinal-archives.Rmd
longitudinal-archives.Rmd
library(onet2r)
library(dplyr)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'dplyr'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     filter, lag
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

Read vignette("longitudinal-onet-background", package = "onet2r") before interpreting changes. O*NET was not designed as a longitudinal panel, so the practical job is not only to compute differences. It is to separate real descriptor updates from carryforwards, re-surveys with stable values, recodes, and taxonomy seams.

This walkthrough uses packaged archive-format text files so every displayed table comes from onet2r functions while the vignette remains CRAN-safe and does not require network access. In a real project, replace the local paths with downloaded O*NET archive versions.

example_archive <- function(version) {
  system.file(
    "extdata",
    "onet-mini",
    paste0("db_", gsub(".", "_", version, fixed = TRUE), "_text"),
    package = "onet2r"
  )
}

archives <- c(
  `30.2` = example_archive("30.2"),
  `30.3` = example_archive("30.3")
)

release_dates <- c(`30.2` = "2026-02-01", `30.3` = "2026-05-01")

archives
#>                                                                                        30.2 
#> "C:/Users/alexfarach/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.5/onet2r/extdata/onet-mini/db_30_2_text" 
#>                                                                                        30.3 
#> "C:/Users/alexfarach/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.5/onet2r/extdata/onet-mini/db_30_3_text"

A Practical Question

Suppose you want to know whether selected cognitive ability ratings changed between two O*NET releases for occupations that matter in workforce planning: software developers, registered nurses, chief executives, and retail supervisors. A naive new - old difference is not enough, because unchanged values may be carryforwards and changed values may reflect a recode rather than a fresh survey update.

Read One Archive Table

onet_archive_read() reads an O*NET text archive table and normalizes it into one panel schema. The local path argument is useful when you have downloaded archives manually or when you want a reproducible, offline workflow.

abilities_303 <- onet_archive_read(
  "30.3",
  "Abilities",
  path = archives[["30.3"]],
  release_date = release_dates[["30.3"]]
)

abilities_303 |>
  select(
    release_version,
    onet_soc_code,
    soc_code,
    element_id,
    element_name,
    data_value,
    source_date,
    domain_source
  ) |>
  head(8) |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 7 × 8
#>   release_version onet_soc_code soc_code element_id element_name       
#>   <chr>           <chr>         <chr>    <chr>      <chr>              
#> 1 30.3            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#> 2 30.3            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#> 3 30.3            29-1141.00    29-1141  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#> 4 30.3            29-1141.00    29-1141  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#> 5 30.3            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#> 6 30.3            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#> 7 30.3            41-1011.00    41-1011  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>   data_value source_date domain_source
#>        <dbl> <date>      <fct>        
#> 1       4.35 2025-07-01  Analyst      
#> 2       4.5  2024-07-01  Analyst      
#> 3       4.71 2025-08-01  Incumbent    
#> 4       4.9  2024-08-01  Incumbent    
#> 5       4.5  2025-07-01  Incumbent    
#> 6       4.22 2024-07-01  Analyst      
#> 7       4.15 2025-06-01  Analyst

The output has one row per occupation, element, scale, and release. The important longitudinal fields are:

  • release_version and release_date, which identify the archive release;
  • soc_vintage, onet_soc_code, and soc_code, which identify the taxonomy;
  • data_value, the descriptor score;
  • source_date and domain_source, which help distinguish fresh updates from carryforwards or method changes.

Assemble a Two-Release Panel

onet_panel() applies the same reader across releases and row-binds the normalized outputs.

panel <- onet_panel(
  "Abilities",
  versions = c("30.2", "30.3"),
  scale = "IM",
  archives = archives,
  release_dates = release_dates
)

panel |>
  arrange(onet_soc_code, element_id, release_version) |>
  select(
    release_version,
    onet_soc_code,
    soc_code,
    element_id,
    element_name,
    data_value,
    source_date,
    domain_source
  ) |>
  head(10) |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 10 × 8
#>    release_version onet_soc_code soc_code element_id element_name       
#>    <chr>           <chr>         <chr>    <chr>      <chr>              
#>  1 30.2            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>  2 30.3            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>  3 30.2            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#>  4 30.3            11-1011.00    11-1011  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#>  5 30.2            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>  6 30.3            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>  7 30.2            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#>  8 30.3            15-1252.00    15-1252  1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensitivity
#>  9 30.2            29-1141.00    29-1141  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#> 10 30.3            29-1141.00    29-1141  1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehension 
#>    data_value source_date domain_source
#>         <dbl> <date>      <fct>        
#>  1       4.38 2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  2       4.5  2025-07-01  Incumbent    
#>  3       4.22 2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  4       4.22 2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  5       4.12 2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  6       4.35 2025-07-01  Analyst      
#>  7       4.5  2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  8       4.5  2024-07-01  Analyst      
#>  9       4.71 2024-08-01  Incumbent    
#> 10       4.71 2025-08-01  Incumbent

This is the table you would save as your audit trail before doing any modeling. It records the exact versions, values, and source dates used in the comparison.

Bridge Taxonomy Vintages

The example releases both use the 2019 O*NET-SOC vintage, so no crosswalk is needed. Calling onet_crosswalk_bridge("2019", "2019") returns the empty bridge schema that onet_panel_reconcile() can use for same-vintage comparisons. For real cross-vintage work, use onet_crosswalk_bridge() to compose official adjacent O*NET-SOC crosswalks.

bridge <- onet_crosswalk_bridge("2019", "2019")
bridge_schema <- tibble::tibble(
  column = names(bridge),
  type = vapply(bridge, \(x) class(x)[[1]], character(1))
)
bridge_schema |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 9 × 2
#>   column           type     
#>   <chr>            <chr>    
#> 1 from_vintage     factor   
#> 2 to_vintage       factor   
#> 3 from_soc_code    character
#> 4 to_soc_code      character
#> 5 from_title       character
#> 6 to_title         character
#> 7 step_count       integer  
#> 8 map_type         factor   
#> 9 crosswalk_weight numeric

Reconcile Adjacent Releases

onet_panel_reconcile() compares adjacent releases and classifies each matched occupation-element-scale pair.

changes <- onet_panel_reconcile(panel, bridge)

changes |>
  select(
    from_soc_code,
    to_soc_code,
    element_name,
    from_value,
    to_value,
    value_change,
    from_source_date,
    to_source_date,
    change_type,
    method_break,
    safely_comparable
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(value_change))) |>
  head(10) |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 7 × 11
#>   from_soc_code to_soc_code element_name        from_value to_value value_change
#>   <chr>         <chr>       <chr>                    <dbl>    <dbl>        <dbl>
#> 1 29-1141       29-1141     Problem Sensitivity       4.6      4.9         0.300
#> 2 15-1252       15-1252     Oral Comprehension        4.12     4.35        0.230
#> 3 41-1011       41-1011     Oral Comprehension        4        4.15        0.150
#> 4 11-1011       11-1011     Oral Comprehension        4.38     4.5         0.120
#> 5 15-1252       15-1252     Problem Sensitivity       4.5      4.5         0    
#> 6 29-1141       29-1141     Oral Comprehension        4.71     4.71        0    
#> 7 11-1011       11-1011     Problem Sensitivity       4.22     4.22        0    
#>   from_source_date to_source_date change_type           method_break
#>   <date>           <date>         <fct>                 <lgl>       
#> 1 2024-08-01       2024-08-01     recode_or_recalc_flag FALSE       
#> 2 2024-07-01       2025-07-01     real_update           FALSE       
#> 3 2024-06-01       2025-06-01     real_update           FALSE       
#> 4 2024-07-01       2025-07-01     real_update           TRUE        
#> 5 2024-07-01       2024-07-01     stale_carryforward    FALSE       
#> 6 2024-08-01       2025-08-01     resampled_stable      FALSE       
#> 7 2024-07-01       2024-07-01     stale_carryforward    FALSE       
#>   safely_comparable
#>   <lgl>            
#> 1 FALSE            
#> 2 TRUE             
#> 3 TRUE             
#> 4 FALSE            
#> 5 TRUE             
#> 6 TRUE             
#> 7 TRUE

Read the change_type column before interpreting value_change:

Value changed? Source date changed? Classification Interpretation
no no stale_carryforward The release likely carried forward the prior value.
yes yes real_update The value changed with a new source date.
no yes resampled_stable The occupation appears updated, but the score stayed stable.
yes no recode_or_recalc_flag Treat cautiously; the value changed without a new source date.

method_break and crosswalk_uncertain are additional overrides. A row is safely_comparable only when the truth-table result is comparable and neither override is present.

Answer the Practical Question

For this small example, the most useful review table is not every pair. It is the subset where values changed, sorted by whether the change is safe to compare.

changes |>
  filter(value_changed) |>
  mutate(abs_change = abs(value_change)) |>
  arrange(safely_comparable, desc(abs_change)) |>
  select(
    to_soc_code,
    element_name,
    from_value,
    to_value,
    value_change,
    change_type,
    method_break,
    safely_comparable
  ) |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 4 × 8
#>   to_soc_code element_name        from_value to_value value_change
#>   <chr>       <chr>                    <dbl>    <dbl>        <dbl>
#> 1 29-1141     Problem Sensitivity       4.6      4.9         0.300
#> 2 11-1011     Oral Comprehension        4.38     4.5         0.120
#> 3 15-1252     Oral Comprehension        4.12     4.35        0.230
#> 4 41-1011     Oral Comprehension        4        4.15        0.150
#>   change_type           method_break safely_comparable
#>   <fct>                 <lgl>        <lgl>            
#> 1 recode_or_recalc_flag FALSE        FALSE            
#> 2 real_update           TRUE         FALSE            
#> 3 real_update           FALSE        TRUE             
#> 4 real_update           FALSE        TRUE

This is the table a researcher should inspect before making a claim such as “software developers require more oral comprehension than in the prior release.” The comparison is strongest when the value changed, the source date changed, and no method or taxonomy warning is present.

Summarize the Panel

onet_change_summary() gives a compact audit of the reconciliation result. The job-family view is useful for spotting whether one part of the SOC taxonomy is driving the apparent change.

onet_change_summary(changes, by = "job_family") |>
  print(width = Inf)
#> # A tibble: 5 × 10
#>   summary_level job_family change_type        n_pairs share_pairs
#>   <chr>         <chr>      <chr>                <int>       <dbl>
#> 1 overall       NA         real_update              7       1    
#> 2 job_family    11         stale_carryforward       2       0.286
#> 3 job_family    15         stale_carryforward       2       0.286
#> 4 job_family    29         resampled_stable         2       0.286
#> 5 job_family    41         real_update              1       0.143
#>   mean_value_change median_abs_value_change share_safely_comparable
#>               <dbl>                   <dbl>                   <dbl>
#> 1            0.114                   0.120                    0.714
#> 2            0.0600                  0.0600                   0.5  
#> 3            0.115                   0.115                    1    
#> 4            0.150                   0.150                    0.5  
#> 5            0.150                   0.150                    1    
#>   share_method_break share_crosswalk_uncertain
#>                <dbl>                     <dbl>
#> 1              0.143                         0
#> 2              0.5                           0
#> 3              0                             0
#> 4              0                             0
#> 5              0                             0

Recommended Workflow for Real Archive Work

  1. Use onet_releases() to identify release versions and O*NET-SOC vintages.
  2. Build a panel with onet_panel() for one domain at a time, such as "Abilities", "Skills", or "Work Activities".
  3. Build a bridge with onet_crosswalk_bridge() when releases use different O*NET-SOC vintages.
  4. Reconcile with onet_panel_reconcile().
  5. Filter, weight, or model only after checking change_type, method_break, crosswalk_uncertain, and safely_comparable.

On this page

Developed by Alex Farach.

Site built with pkgdown 2.1.3.
